stem: Consider the linear system \( A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b} \) with \[ A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & [[s]] & [[t]] \\ [[p]] & [[m22]] & [[m23]] \\ [[q]] & [[m32]] & [[m33]] \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} [[b1]] \\ [[b2]] \\ [[b3]] \end{bmatrix}. \] As the first step in deciding whether the system is consistent, what is the rank of the coefficient matrix \( A \)?
solution: Row-reducing \( A \) (ignoring \( \mathbf{b} \) for now) shows it has \( [[answer]] \) pivot rows, so \( \operatorname{rank}(A) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the nullity \(3-\operatorname{rank}(A)\), not the rank itself.
This overcounts the pivot rows by one.
This undercounts the pivot rows by one.
This overcounts the pivot rows, which cannot exceed the number of rows or columns.
This assumes \(A\) is the zero matrix.
This assumes \(A\) has full rank without checking for dependence among its rows.

stem: Consider the linear system \( A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b} \) with \[ A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & [[s]] & [[t]] \\ [[p]] & [[m22]] & [[m23]] \\ [[q]] & [[m32]] & [[m33]] \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} [[b1]] \\ [[b2]] \\ [[b3]] \end{bmatrix}. \] To check consistency, form the augmented matrix \( [A \mid \mathbf{b}] \). What is the rank of \( [A \mid \mathbf{b}] \)?
solution: Row-reducing the augmented matrix \( [A\mid \mathbf{b}] \) shows it has \( [[answer]] \) pivot rows, so \( \operatorname{rank}([A\mid\mathbf{b}]) = [[answer]] \). (The system is consistent exactly when this equals \( \operatorname{rank}(A) \).)
distractor_rationales: This subtracts the rank from the ambient dimension \(3\) instead of reporting it directly.
This overcounts the pivot rows by one, more than the number of rows allows.
This undercounts the pivot rows by one.
This undercounts the pivot rows by two.
This assumes the augmented matrix is entirely zero.
This overcounts the rank, which cannot exceed the number of rows (3), regardless of the number of columns.

stem: The linear system \( A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b} \), with \[ A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & [[s]] & [[t]] \\ [[p]] & [[m22]] & [[m23]] \\ [[q]] & [[m32]] & [[m33]] \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} [[bb1]] \\ [[bb2]] \\ [[bb3]] \end{bmatrix}, \] is known to be consistent. What is the dimension of its solution set (the number of free parameters in the general solution)?
solution: Since the system is consistent, the solution set is a coset of \( \operatorname{Null}(A) \), of dimension \( 3 - \operatorname{rank}(A) \). Row-reducing \( A \) gives \( \operatorname{rank}(A) = [[rankval]] \), so the solution set has dimension \( 3 - [[rankval]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( \operatorname{rank}(A) \), not the dimension of the solution set.
This assumes \(A\) is the zero matrix, so every variable would be free.
This overcounts the free parameters by one.
This undercounts the free parameters by one.
This is a plausible but unverified count.
This is a plausible but unverified count.
This assumes \(A\) is invertible, giving a unique solution.

stem: A linear system \( A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b} \) has \( [[m]] \) equations in \( [[n]] \) unknowns. The coefficient matrix \( A \) has rank \( [[rA]] \), and the augmented matrix \( [A\mid\mathbf{b}] \) also has rank \( [[rA]] \) (so the system is consistent). What is the dimension of the solution set?
solution: Since \( \operatorname{rank}(A) = \operatorname{rank}([A\mid\mathbf{b}]) = [[rA]] \), the system is consistent, and its solution set has dimension \( n - \operatorname{rank}(A) = [[n]] - [[rA]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the number of equations \(m\) instead of the number of unknowns \(n\).
This reports the rank itself instead of subtracting it from the number of unknowns.
This subtracts the number of equations from the number of unknowns, ignoring the given rank.
This adds the rank instead of subtracting it.
This is off by one from the correct dimension.
This is off by one from the correct dimension.
This assumes the rank is \(0\), reporting the number of equations.
This assumes the rank is \(0\), reporting the full number of unknowns.
